subject: Mathematics
q: An ellipse passes through the foci of the hyperbola, <math><mn>9</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>36</mn></math> and its major and minor axes lie along the transverse and conjugate axes of the hyperbola respectively. If the product of eccentricities of the two conics is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then which of the following points does not lie on the ellipse?
question: An ellipse passes through the foci of the hyperbola, <math><mn>9</mn><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>36</mn></math> and its major and minor axes lie along the transverse and conjugate axes of the hyperbola respectively. If the product of eccentricities of the two conics is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>,</mo></math> then which of the following points does not lie on the ellipse?
solution:
Equation of the hyperbola
x24-y29=1



Focus of hyperbola a1e1, 0 and (-a1e1, 0)  

a1=2, e1= 1+94=132

∴ Foci would be +13, 0 and -13, 0 

Product of eccentricity would be 

132 ⋅e2=12

∴  e2= 113 .

As the major & minor axis of the ellipse coincide with axis of hyperbola then the value of a2 for ellipse would be 13 ,

e2=1-b22a22

113=1-b2213

b22=12

∴ Equation of the ellipse would be 

x213+y212=1.

Option (1)  394 ⋅(13)+312=1

Satisfies the equation, hence it lies on the ellipse. 

Option (2) 134 (13)+34.12=1

does not lie on the ellipse.

Option (3)  132(13)+612=1 satisfy.

Option (4) 1313+0=1 satisfy.

So, option 132, 32 is the answer.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the length of a latus rectum of an ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ be 10 . If its eccentricity is the minimum value of the function $f(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{t}^2+\mathrm{t}+\frac{11}{12}$, $\mathrm{t} \in \mathbf{R}$, then $\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2$ is equal to :
question: Let the length of a latus rectum of an ellipse $\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1$ be 10 . If its eccentricity is the minimum value of the function $f(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{t}^2+\mathrm{t}+\frac{11}{12}$, $\mathrm{t} \in \mathbf{R}$, then $\mathrm{a}^2+\mathrm{b}^2$ is equal to :
solution:
Length of LR $=\frac{2 b^2}{a}=10 \Rightarrow 5 \mathrm{a}=\mathrm{b}^2$...(1)
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{t})=\mathrm{t}^2+\mathrm{t}+\frac{11}{12} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{df}(\mathrm{t})}{\mathrm{dt}}=2 \mathrm{t}+1=0 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\frac{-1}{2} \\ & \text { Min value of } \mathrm{f}(\mathrm{t})=\left(\frac{-1}{2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{-1}{2}\right)+\frac{11}{12} \\ & =\frac{1}{4} \frac{-1}{2}+\frac{11}{12}=\frac{3-6+11}{12}=\frac{8}{12}=\frac{2}{3}=\mathrm{e} \\ & \mathrm{e}^2=\frac{1-\mathrm{b}^2}{\mathrm{a}^2} \Rightarrow \frac{4}{9}=\frac{1-\mathrm{b}^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{b}^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}=\frac{1-4}{\mathrm{a}}=\frac{5}{\mathrm{a}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{b}^2=\frac{5 \mathrm{a}^2}{\mathrm{a}}$...(2)
$\begin{aligned} & \text { From (1) \& (2) } \\ & 5 \mathrm{a}=\frac{5 \mathrm{a}^2}{\mathrm{a}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=9, \quad \mathrm{~b}=\sqrt{45}=3 \sqrt{5} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{a} 2+\mathrm{b} 2=81+45=126\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: An ellipse has $\mathrm{OB}$ as semi minor axis, $\mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{\prime}$ its focii and the angle FBF' is a right angle. Then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
question: An ellipse has $\mathrm{OB}$ as semi minor axis, $\mathrm{F}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{\prime}$ its focii and the angle FBF' is a right angle. Then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
solution: $$ \begin{aligned} & \because \angle \text { FBF }^{\prime}=90^{\circ} \\ & \therefore\left(\sqrt{a^2 e^2+b^2}\right)^2+\left(\sqrt{a^2 e^2+b^2}\right)^2=(2 a \mathrm{e})^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2\left(a^2 e^2+b^2\right)=4 a^2 e^2 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}^2=\mathrm{b}^2 / \mathrm{a}^2 \\ & \text { Also } \mathrm{e}^2=1-\mathrm{b}^2 / \mathrm{a}^2=1-\mathrm{e}^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 2 \mathrm{e}^2=1, \mathrm{e}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} . \end{aligned} $$

subject: Mathematics
q: The locus of the mid-point of the line segment joining the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the points on the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is an ellipse with eccentricity
question: The locus of the mid-point of the line segment joining the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the points on the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is an ellipse with eccentricity
solution:
Given,

x24+y22=1

Let coordinate of D 2cosθ+42,2sinθ+32≡h,k

So, 2h-42=cosθ      ...1

And 2k-32=sinθ      ...2

Equation 12+equation 22, then we get

2h-422+2k-322=1⇒x-221+y-32212=1

∴  Required eccentricity is e=1-12=12

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the length of the latus rectum of an ellipse with its major axis along $x$ -axis and centre at the origin, be 8 . If the distance between the foci of this ellipse is equal to the length of its minor axis, then which one of the following points lies on it?
question: Let the length of the latus rectum of an ellipse with its major axis along $x$ -axis and centre at the origin, be 8 . If the distance between the foci of this ellipse is equal to the length of its minor axis, then which one of the following points lies on it?
solution:
Let the ellipse be $\frac{x^{2}}{a^{2}}+\frac{y^{2}}{b^{2}}=1$

Then, $\frac{2 b^{2}}{a}=8,2 a e-b^{2}$ and $b^{2}=a^{3}\left(1-e^{2}\right)$

$\Rightarrow a=8, b^{2}=32$

Then, the equation of the ellipse

$\frac{x^{2}}{64}+\frac{y^{2}}{32}=1$

Hence, the point $(4 \sqrt{3}, 2 \sqrt{2})$ lies on the ellipse.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the midpoint of a chord of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$ is $(\sqrt{2}, 4 / 3)$, and the length of the chord is $\frac{2 \sqrt{\alpha}}{3}$, then $\alpha$ is :
question: If the midpoint of a chord of the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$ is $(\sqrt{2}, 4 / 3)$, and the length of the chord is $\frac{2 \sqrt{\alpha}}{3}$, then $\alpha$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & E: \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1 \\ & T=S_1 \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\sqrt{2} x}{9}+\frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{4}{3} y\right)-1=\frac{2}{9}+\frac{16}{9(4)}-1 \\ & \frac{\sqrt{2} x}{9}+\frac{y}{3}=\frac{2}{9}+\frac{4}{9} \\ & \frac{\sqrt{2} x}{9}+\frac{y}{3}=\frac{2}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \sqrt{2 x}+3 y=6 \end{aligned}$ Now point of intersection of chord and ellipse is $\begin{aligned} & \frac{(6-3 y)^2}{18}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1 \\ & \frac{(2-y)^2}{2}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1 \\ & 2\left(4+y^2-4 y\right)+y^2=4 \\ & \Rightarrow 3 y^2-8 y+4=0 \\ & \Rightarrow y=2, \frac{2}{3} \end{aligned}$ So, points are $(0,2)$ are $\left(2 \sqrt{2}, \frac{2}{3}\right)$ Length of chord $=\sqrt{(2 \sqrt{2})^2+\left(\frac{2}{3}-2\right)^2}$ $\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{8+\frac{16}{9}} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{88}}{3}=\frac{2 \sqrt{22}}{3} \end{aligned}$ On comparing $\alpha=22$

q: If the co-ordinates of two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>7</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>-</mo><msqrt><mn>7</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> respectively and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is any point on the conic, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>16</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>144</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is equal to :
question: If the co-ordinates of two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><msqrt><mn>7</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> and  <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mo>-</mo><msqrt><mn>7</mn></msqrt><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>)</mo></math> respectively and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is any point on the conic, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>9</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>16</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>144</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>A</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>P</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is equal to :
solution:
For ellipse x216+y29=1

Comparing with, standard equation of ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1, we get  

a=4,b=3.

∴e=1-916=74
Since, A and B are foci

Therefore, PA+PB=2a

=2×4=8.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let A be the point $(3, 0)$ and circles with variable diameter AB touch the circle $x^2 + y^2 = 36$ internally. Let the curve C be the locus of the point B. If the eccentricity of C is $e$, then $72e^2$ is equal to _______.
question: Let A be the point $(3, 0)$ and circles with variable diameter AB touch the circle $x^2 + y^2 = 36$ internally. Let the curve C be the locus of the point B. If the eccentricity of C is $e$, then $72e^2$ is equal to _______.
solution:
Let the coordinates of point B be $(h, k)$.

The center of the circle with diameter AB is $C_1 \left( \dfrac{h+3}{2}, \dfrac{k}{2} \right)$ and its radius is $r_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{(h-3)^2 + k^2}$.

The given circle is $x^2 + y^2 = 36$, which has center $C_2(0, 0)$ and radius $r_2 = 6$.

Since the circles touch internally, the distance between their centers is equal to the difference of their radii:

$C_1C_2 = r_2 - r_1$

$\sqrt{\left(\dfrac{h+3}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{k}{2}\right)^2} = 6 - \dfrac{1}{2} \sqrt{(h-3)^2 + k^2}$

Multiplying the entire equation by 2, we get:

$\sqrt{(h+3)^2 + k^2} + \sqrt{(h-3)^2 + k^2} = 12$

This equation represents the locus of a point $B(h, k)$ such that the sum of its distances from two fixed points $S_1(-3, 0)$ and $S_2(3, 0)$ is constant and equal to $12$. This is the standard definition of an ellipse.

Thus, the foci of the ellipse are $(\pm 3, 0)$ and the length of the major axis is $2a = 12$.

The distance between the foci is $2ae = 6$.

Substituting $2a = 12$, we get $12e = 6 \Rightarrow e = \dfrac{1}{2}$.

We need to find the value of $72e^2$:

$72e^2 = 72 \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^2 = 72 \times \dfrac{1}{4} = 18$

Answer: $18$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let C be the largest circle centred at 2,0 and inscribed in the ellipse x236+y216=1. If 1,α lies on C, then 10α2 is equal to ______
question: Let C be the largest circle centred at 2,0 and inscribed in the ellipse x236+y216=1. If 1,α lies on C, then 10α2 is equal to ______
solution:
Given:

x236+y216=1

Let any point on the ellipse be P6cosθ, 4sinθ.

Equation of normal of ellipse at any point P is

3secθx–2cosecθy=10

This normal is also the normal of the circle passing through the point 2,0.

So, 6secθ=10 or sinθ=0 (Not possible)

cosθ=35 and sinθ=45 

So, point P≡185,165.

So, the largest radius of circle r=3205=645

So the equation of circle x-22+y2=645

This circle is passing through 1,α, hence

1-22+α2=645

⇒α2=595

⇒10α2=118.

q: A focus of an ellipse is at the origin. The directrix is the line $x=4$ and the eccentricity is $1 / 2$. Then the length of the semi-major axis is
question: A focus of an ellipse is at the origin. The directrix is the line $x=4$ and the eccentricity is $1 / 2$. Then the length of the semi-major axis is
solution:
Major axis is along $x$-axis.
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{a}{e}-a e=4 \\
& a\left(2-\frac{1}{2}\right)=4 \\
& a=\frac{8}{3} .
\end{aligned}
$$

q: An ellipse, with foci at <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0,2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi> </mi></math> and minor axis of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> , passes through which of the following points?
question: An ellipse, with foci at <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0,2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi> </mi></math> and minor axis of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> , passes through which of the following points?

(here a<b )
∵ a2=b21-e2
∴b2=8
∴ Equation of ellipse x24+y28=1
Clearly 2, 2 lies on the ellipse.

subject: Mathematics
q: An ellipse has its center at $(1,-2)$, one focus at $(3,-2)$ and one vertex at $(5,-2)$. Then the length of its latus rectum is :
question: An ellipse has its center at $(1,-2)$, one focus at $(3,-2)$ and one vertex at $(5,-2)$. Then the length of its latus rectum is :
solution:
Center at $(1, -2)$, focus at $(3, -2)$, vertex at $(5, -2)$ lie on horizontal line.
Distance from center to focus: $c = 2$.
Distance from center to vertex: $a = 4$.
Since $b^2 = a^2 - c^2 = 16 - 4 = 12$.
Length of latus rectum: $L = \dfrac{2b^2}{a} = \dfrac{2(12)}{4} = 6$

q: If the distance between the foci of an ellipse is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> and the distance between its directrix is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the length of its latus rectum is
question: If the distance between the foci of an ellipse is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> and the distance between its directrix is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo>,</mo></math> then the length of its latus rectum is
solution:
Given, 2ae=6 and 2ae=12, directrices are x=±ae

So, ae=6

Now, using the formula for eccentricity of an ellipse

⇒b2=a2-a2e2=18-9=9

⇒L.R.=2b2a=2×932=32

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the ellipse \(\mathrm{E}_1: \frac{x^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}+\frac{y^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1, \mathrm{a}\gt\mathrm{b}\) and \(\mathrm{E}_2: \frac{x^2}{\mathrm{~A}^2}+\frac{y^2}{\mathrm{~B}^2}=1, \mathrm{~A} \lt \mathrm{B}\) have same eccentricity \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\). Let the product of their lengths of latus rectums be \(\frac{32}{\sqrt{3}}\), and the distance between the foci of \(E_1\) be 4. If \(E_1\) and \(E_2\) meet at \(A, B, C\) and \(D\), then the area of the quadrilateral \(A B C D\) equals :
question: Let the ellipse \(\mathrm{E}_1: \frac{x^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}+\frac{y^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1, \mathrm{a}\gt\mathrm{b}\) and \(\mathrm{E}_2: \frac{x^2}{\mathrm{~A}^2}+\frac{y^2}{\mathrm{~B}^2}=1, \mathrm{~A} \lt \mathrm{B}\) have same eccentricity \(\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\). Let the product of their lengths of latus rectums be \(\frac{32}{\sqrt{3}}\), and the distance between the foci of \(E_1\) be 4. If \(E_1\) and \(E_2\) meet at \(A, B, C\) and \(D\), then the area of the quadrilateral \(A B C D\) equals :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & 2 a e=4 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad a=2 \sqrt{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 1-\frac{b^2}{12}=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow b^2=8 \\ & \\ & \frac{2 b^2}{a} \times \frac{2 A^2}{B}=\frac{32}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & \Rightarrow \\ & \frac{2 \times 8}{2 \sqrt{3}} \times \frac{2 A^2}{B}=\frac{32}{\sqrt{3}} \\ & \Rightarrow \\ & \frac{A^2}{B}=2 \Rightarrow A^2=2 B \\ & \\ & \quad 1-\frac{A^2}{B}=\frac{1}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \\ & B=3 \Rightarrow A^2=6\end{aligned}$
$E_1: \frac{x^2}{12}+\frac{y^2}{8}=1$ ....(i)
$E_1: \frac{x^2}{6}+\frac{y^2}{9}=1$...(ii)
On solving (i) \& (ii)
$\begin{aligned} &(x, y)=\left(\frac{\sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{5}}\right),\left(\frac{-\sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{6}{\sqrt{5}}\right),\left(\frac{\sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{-6}{\sqrt{5}}\right), \\ &\left(\frac{-\sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{5}}, \frac{-6}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\end{aligned}$
Four points are vertices of rectangle area $=\frac{24 \sqrt{6}}{5}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let S and $\mathrm{S}^{\prime}$ be the foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{25}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1$ and $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, \beta)$ be a point on the ellipse in the first quadrant. If $(\mathrm{SP})^{2}+\left(\mathrm{S}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}\right)^{2}-\mathrm{SP} \cdot \mathrm{S}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}=37$, then $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}$ is equal to :
question: Let S and $\mathrm{S}^{\prime}$ be the foci of the ellipse $\frac{x^{2}}{25}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}=1$ and $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, \beta)$ be a point on the ellipse in the first quadrant. If $(\mathrm{SP})^{2}+\left(\mathrm{S}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}\right)^{2}-\mathrm{SP} \cdot \mathrm{S}^{\prime} \mathrm{P}=37$, then $\alpha^{2}+\beta^{2}$ is equal to :
solution:
For the ellipse $\frac{x^2}{25} + \frac{y^2}{9} = 1$, we have $a = 5$, $b = 3$, $c = 4$.
So foci are at $S(\pm 4, 0)$.
For point $P$ on the ellipse: $SP + S'P = 10$.
Given $(SP)^2 + (S'P)^2 - SP \cdot S'P = 37$
Let $r_1 = SP$ and $r_2 = S'P$.
From $(r_1 + r_2)^2 = 100$, we get $r_1^2 + r_2^2 = 100 - 2r_1r_2$.
Substituting into the given equation: $100 - 3r_1r_2 = 37$, so $r_1r_2 = 21$.
Thus $r_1 = 3, r_2 = 7$.
From $(\alpha + 4)^2 + \beta^2 = 9$ and $(\alpha - 4)^2 + \beta^2 = 49$, subtracting gives $16\alpha = -40$, so $\alpha = -\frac{5}{2}$.
From the ellipse equation: $\beta^2 = \frac{27}{4}$.
Therefore $\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = \frac{25}{4} + \frac{27}{4} = 13$.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the normal to the ellipse <math><msup><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn></math> at a point <math><mi>P</mi></math> on it is parallel to the line, <math><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and the tangent to the ellipse at <math><mi>P</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>Q</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>4,4</mn><mo>)</mo></math> then <math><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> is equal to:
question: If the normal to the ellipse <math><msup><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>12</mn></math> at a point <math><mi>P</mi></math> on it is parallel to the line, <math><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and the tangent to the ellipse at <math><mi>P</mi></math> passes through <math><mi>Q</mi><mo>(</mo><mn>4,4</mn><mo>)</mo></math> then <math><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Given the equation of an ellipse is x24+y23=1,where a=2, b=3.
As we know equation of normal to the ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1 can be taken as
axsec⁡θ-bycosec⁡θ=a2-b2
⇒2xsec⁡θ-3ycosec⁡θ=1 for the given ellipse.
Now, this normal is parallel to 2x+y=5.
⇒-2sec⁡θ3cosec⁡θ=-2⇒tan⁡θ=-3⇒θ=2π3,-π3
Hence point Pacos⁡θ, bsin⁡θ=-1,32 or 1, -32
Tangent at -1,32 is x.-14+y.323=1
⇒-x+2y=4 which also passes through Q4, 4
⇒PQ=4±12+4∓322=552.

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let the equations of two ellipses be
$$
E_1: \frac{x^2}{3}+\frac{y^2}{2}=1 \text { and } E_2: \frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1 \text {, }
$$
If the product of their eccentricities is $\frac{1}{2}$, then the length of the minor axis of ellipse $E_2$ is :
question:
Let the equations of two ellipses be
$$
E_1: \frac{x^2}{3}+\frac{y^2}{2}=1 \text { and } E_2: \frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1 \text {, }
$$
If the product of their eccentricities is $\frac{1}{2}$, then the length of the minor axis of ellipse $E_2$ is :
solution:
Given equations of ellipses
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}_1: \frac{x^2}{3}+\frac{y^2}{2}=1 \\
& \Rightarrow e_1=\sqrt{1-\frac{2}{3}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \\
& \text { and } \mathrm{E}_2: \frac{x^2}{16}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=1 \\
& \Rightarrow e_2=\sqrt{\frac{1-b^2}{16}}=\sqrt{\frac{16-b^2}{4}} \\
& \text { Also, given } e_1 \times e_2=\frac{1}{2} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \times \sqrt{\frac{16-b^2}{4}}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow 16-b^2=12 \\
& \Rightarrow b^2=4 \\
& \therefore \text { Length of minor axis of } \\
& \mathrm{E}_2=2 b=2 \times 2=4
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mo>></mo><mi>b</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be another ellipse such that it touches the end points of major axis of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and the foci of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are the end points of minor axis of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> have same eccentricities, then its value is:
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>:</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></mrow><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>a</mi><mo>></mo><mi>b</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> be another ellipse such that it touches the end points of major axis of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and the foci of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are the end points of minor axis of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> have same eccentricities, then its value is:
solution:
Given ellipse is E1:x2a2+y2b2=1, a>b and E2 is another ellipse which touches the end points of the major axis of E1 and foci of E2 are at the minor axis of E1, thus the two ellipses are given by the following diagram.

Since, the ellipse E2 touches the major axis of the ellipse E1, hence, the minor axis of the ellipse E2 is a1=a.

Now, let the major axis of the ellipse E2 is b1=c and it is obvious from the diagram and the given conditions that b1>a1.

We know that the eccentricity of an ellipse x2A2+y2B2=1, A>B is 1-B2A2.

Thus, for the ellipse E1, we have e=1-b2a2 and for the ellipse E2, we have e1=1-a2c2.

Given, e=e1

1-b2a2=1-a2c2

⇒ 1-b2a2=1-a2c2

⇒ b2a2=a2c2

⇒ c2=a4b2

⇒ c=a2b   ...i

Also, given that the foci of E2 are the end points of the minor axis of E1, thus b=ce

⇒ c=be   ...ii

From the above two equations, we get be=a2 b

⇒ e=b2a2

Now, using the definition of eccentricity, we get e=b2a2=1-e2

⇒ e2+e-1=0

Now, applying the Sridharacharya's formula for the roots of a quadratic equation, i.e. if ax2+bx+c=0, a≠0, then x=-b±b2-4ac2a, we get

e=-1±12-4×1×-12×1

⇒ e=-1±1+42

⇒ e=-1±52

But, eccentricity can never be negative, hence e=-1+52.

q: If the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> intersects the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> at two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, then the angle subtended by the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> at the origin is
question: If the curve <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> intersects the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> at two points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, then the angle subtended by the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> at the origin is
solution:
Homogenising

x2+2y2-2x+y2=0

⇒-x2-4xy=0⇒x2+4xy=0

Lines are x=0 and y=-x4

∴ Angle between lines =π2+tan-114

subject: Mathematics
q: Let a focus of the ellipse $E: \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ be $S(4, 0)$ and its eccentricity be $\dfrac{4}{5}$. If the point $P(3, \alpha)$ lies on $E$ and $O$ is the origin, then the area of $\triangle POS$ is equal to:
question: Let a focus of the ellipse $E: \dfrac{x^2}{a^2} + \dfrac{y^2}{b^2} = 1$ be $S(4, 0)$ and its eccentricity be $\dfrac{4}{5}$. If the point $P(3, \alpha)$ lies on $E$ and $O$ is the origin, then the area of $\triangle POS$ is equal to:
solution:
Given the focus of the ellipse $S(4, 0)$, we have $ae = 4$.

Since the eccentricity $e = \dfrac{4}{5}$, we get $a \left(\dfrac{4}{5}\right) = 4 \Rightarrow a = 5$.

Using the relation $b^2 = a^2(1 - e^2)$, we find:

$b^2 = 25 \left(1 - \dfrac{16}{25}\right) = 9$

The equation of the ellipse is $\dfrac{x^2}{25} + \dfrac{y^2}{9} = 1$.

Since the point $P(3, \alpha)$ lies on the ellipse, substituting $x = 3$ gives:

$\dfrac{9}{25} + \dfrac{\alpha^2}{9} = 1$

$\dfrac{\alpha^2}{9} = 1 - \dfrac{9}{25} = \dfrac{16}{25}$

$\alpha^2 = \dfrac{144}{25} \Rightarrow |\alpha| = \dfrac{12}{5}$

The coordinates of the vertices of $\triangle POS$ are $O(0, 0)$, $S(4, 0)$, and $P\left(3, \pm \dfrac{12}{5}\right)$.

The area of $\triangle POS$ is $\dfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$.

Taking $OS$ as the base, the length is $4$, and the height is the absolute value of the y-coordinate of $P$, which is $\dfrac{12}{5}$.

Area $= \dfrac{1}{2} \times 4 \times \dfrac{12}{5} = \dfrac{24}{5}$

Answer: $24/5$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>S</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math> be the foci of an ellipse and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> be any one of the extremities of its minor axis. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><msup><mi>S</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mi>B</mi><mi>S</mi></math> is a right angled triangle with right angle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>Δ</mi><msup><mi>S</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mi>B</mi><mi>S</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sq</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>units</mi></math>, then the length of a latus rectum of the ellipse is :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>S</mi><mo>'</mo></msup></math> be the foci of an ellipse and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> be any one of the extremities of its minor axis. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Δ</mi><msup><mi>S</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mi>B</mi><mi>S</mi></math> is a right angled triangle with right angle at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and area <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>Δ</mi><msup><mi>S</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mi>B</mi><mi>S</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo> </mo><mi>sq</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>units</mi></math>, then the length of a latus rectum of the ellipse is :
solution:
Given that, 

∠SBS'=π2

⇒∠OBS=π4

⇒b=ae  ...i 

Also, area ∆SBS'=8 

⇒12.2ae.b=8

⇒aeb=8  ...ii 

From equations i & ii, 

b2=8 and a2=b2+a2e2=8+8=16

⇒b=22 and a=4

Thus, the length of the latus rectum is 2b2a=164=4

subject: Mathematics
q: If a tangent to the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> meets the tangents at the extremities of its major axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the circle with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> as diameter passes through the point.
question: If a tangent to the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> meets the tangents at the extremities of its major axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then the circle with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> as diameter passes through the point.
solution:
We have, x24+y21=1

Equation of tangent is cosθx+2sinθy=2

B-2,1+cosθsinθ,C2,1-cosθsinθ

≡B-2,cotθ2,C2,tanθ2

Equation of required circle will be

x+2x-2+y-cotθ2y-tanθ2=0

⇒x2-4+y2-tanθ2+cotθ2y+1=0

Hence, 3,0 satisfies the above equation.

subject: Mathematics
q: If the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> meets the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>7</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>y</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>6</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>7</mn></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>y</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>6</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
question: If the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> meets the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>7</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>y</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>6</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis and the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>7</mn></mfrac><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mi>y</mi><mrow><mn>2</mn><msqrt><mn>6</mn></msqrt></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math>-axis, then the eccentricity of the ellipse is
solution:
Given,

x2a2+y2b2=1 meets the line x7+y26=1 on the x-axis, so at x-axis y=0 putting in both equation and comparing we get, a=7 

And x2a2+y2b2=1 meets the line x7-y26=1 on the y-axis where x=0 so putting in both equation and comparing we get, b=26

Therefore, e2=1-b2a2=1-2449

⇒e=2549=57

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $\mathrm{E}_1: \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$ be an ellipse. Ellipses $\mathrm{E}_1$ 's are constructed such that their centres and eccentricities are same as that of $E_1$, and the length of minor axis of $E_i$ is the length of major axis of $E_{i+1}(i \geq 1)$. If $A_i$ is the area of the ellipse $E_i$, then $\frac{5}{\pi}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right)$, is equal to
question: Let $\mathrm{E}_1: \frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$ be an ellipse. Ellipses $\mathrm{E}_1$ 's are constructed such that their centres and eccentricities are same as that of $E_1$, and the length of minor axis of $E_i$ is the length of major axis of $E_{i+1}(i \geq 1)$. If $A_i$ is the area of the ellipse $E_i$, then $\frac{5}{\pi}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{\infty} A_i\right)$, is equal to
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}_1=\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{9}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{4} \Rightarrow \mathrm{e}=\sqrt{1-\frac{4}{9}}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3} \\ & \mathrm{E}_2: \frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\mathrm{a}^2}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{4}=1 \\ & \mathrm{e}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}=\sqrt{1-\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{4}} \Rightarrow \frac{5}{9}=1-\frac{\mathrm{a}^2}{4} \\ & \mathrm{a}^2=\frac{16}{9}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}_2: \frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\frac{16}{9}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{4}=1 \\ & \mathrm{E}_3: \frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\frac{16}{9}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{\mathrm{~b}^2}=1 \\ & \mathrm{e}=\frac{\sqrt{5}}{3}=\sqrt{1-\frac{\mathrm{b}^2}{\frac{16}{9}}} \Rightarrow b^2=\frac{64}{81}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E}_3=\frac{\mathrm{x}^2}{\frac{16}{9}}+\frac{\mathrm{y}^2}{\frac{64}{81}}=1 \\ & \mathrm{~A}_1=\pi \times 3 \times 2 \Rightarrow 6 \pi \\ & \mathrm{~A}_2=\pi \times \frac{4}{3} \times 2=\frac{8 \pi}{3} \\ & \mathrm{~A}_3=\pi \times \frac{4}{3} \times \frac{8}{9}=\frac{32 \pi}{81} \\ & \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\infty} \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{i}}=6 \pi+\frac{8 \pi}{3}+\frac{32 \pi}{81}+\ldots . \infty \Rightarrow \frac{6 \pi}{1-\frac{4}{9}} \Rightarrow \frac{54 \pi}{5} \\ & \therefore \frac{5}{\pi} \sum_{\mathrm{i}=1}^{\infty} \mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{i}} \Rightarrow \frac{5}{\pi} \times \frac{54 \pi}{5}=54\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the length of the latus rectum of an ellipse is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> units and the distance between a focus and its nearest vertex on the major axis is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> units, then its eccentricity is
question: If the length of the latus rectum of an ellipse is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> units and the distance between a focus and its nearest vertex on the major axis is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> units, then its eccentricity is
solution:
Given length of latus-rectum of an ellipse is 2b2a=4⇒b2=2a and  a-ae=32⇒a1-e=32.

Now we know, b2=a21-e2

⇒b2=a1-ea1+e

⇒2a=32a1+e  ∵  a1-e=32

⇒43=1+e

⇒e=13.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> be the acute angle between the tangents to the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>9</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>1</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> at their point of intersection in the first quadrant. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mi>θ</mi></math> is equal to :
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> be the acute angle between the tangents to the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>9</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>1</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> and the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn></math> at their point of intersection in the first quadrant. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>tan</mi><mi>θ</mi></math> is equal to :
solution:
Given, x29+y21=1   ...1

x2+y2=3   ...2

On solving equations 1 & 2, we get

x29+3-x21=1

⇒x2+27-9x2=9

⇒8x2=18

⇒x2=94⇒x=±32

⇒x=32 

Since, point of intersection in the first quadrant.

Put, x=32 in equation 2, we get y=32. 

Hence, the point of intersection of the curves x29+y21=1 and x2+y2=3 in the first quadrant is 32, 32

Now, differentiate the equation 1 on both sides with respect to x, we get

y'=-x9y

∴Slope of tangent to the ellipse x29+y21=1 at 32, 32 is m1=-133

Now, differentiate the equation 2 on both sides with respect to x, we get

y'=-xy

∴Slope of tangent to the circle x2+y2=3 at 32, 32 is m2=-3

So, if acute angle between both curves is θ then  tanθ=m1-m21+m1m2=-133+31+-133(-3)=23

subject: Mathematics
q: If the tangent to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math> at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is also a tangent to the ellipse, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> then <math><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to:
question: If the tangent to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math> at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>β</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is also a tangent to the ellipse, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> then <math><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to:
solution:
Since α, β lies on the parabola ⇒α=β2

⇒α, β=β2, β

Tangent to a parabola y2=4ax at a point x1, y1 is yy1=2ax+x1

Therefore, tangent to the parabola y2=x at β2, β isy⋅β=12x+β2

⇒y=x2β+β2   ...i

Tangent to parabola is also a tangent to ellipse

x2+2y2=1 ⇒x2+y212=1

And, a line y=mx+c is a tangent to the ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1 if c2=a2m2+b2

Using the condition c2=a2m2+b2, we get

β24=1212β2+12

⇒β24=14β2+12

⇒β4-2β2-1=0

Using Sridharacharya's Formula the roots of a quadratic equation ax2+bx+c=0, a≠0 are x=-b±b2-4ac2a

⇒β2=--2±-22-4×1×-12×1

⇒β2=2±82

⇒β2=1+2 or β2=1-2 (Neglected as β2 is positive)

⇒β2=2+1

⇒α=2+1.

subject: Mathematics
q: The tangent and normal to the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>32</mn></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> meet the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, respectively. Then the area (in sq. units) of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is:
question: The tangent and normal to the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>32</mn></math> at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> meet the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>, respectively. Then the area (in sq. units) of the triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi><mi>R</mi></math> is:
solution:
The equation of tangent and normal to the ellipse x2a2+y2b2=1 at a point x1, y1 are respectively xx1a2+yy1b2=1 and a2xx1-b2yy1=a2-b2.

The given ellipse is 3x2+5y2=32,  ⇒3x232+5y232=1.

Hence, the tangent and normal to the ellipse 3x232+5y232=1 at 2, 2 are respectively 3×2×x32+5×2×y32=1 and 32×x3×2-32×y5×2=323-325

Hence, the tangent is 3x+5y=16   ...1

And, the normal is 5x-3y=4     ...2

The point where the tangent meets the x-axis can be obtained by putting y=0, ⇒3x+0=16  ⇒x=163, thus the point Q≡163, 0.

Similarly, the point where the normal meets the x-axis, is x=45, thus the point R≡43, 0. 

Tangent and normal intersect at P2, 2, and the length of perpendicular from any point on x-axis is the absolute value of its y-co-ordinate, hence the height of the triangle is 2 units.

And, hence the required area is =12QR×h

=12×163-45×2=6815 sq units.
